title: Difference Array
description: The mirror-image trick of Prefix Sum -- applying many range UPDATES efficiently instead of answering many range sum QUERIES.
objectives: Understand difference array as the inverse problem of prefix sum
Apply a range update in O(1) using a difference array
Reconstruct the final array with a prefix sum pass

sections.text: Prefix Sum solved: many range-sum QUERIES on a fixed array. Difference array solves the opposite kind of problem: many range UPDATES ("add 5 to every element from index 2 to 5") applied to an array, where we only care about the FINAL result after all updates.
Each direct update is O(range length). Applying q such updates costs O(q * n) in the worst case -- exactly the same shape of problem prefix sum solved, just for updates instead of queries.
An auxiliary array where each position holds the DIFFERENCE between consecutive elements of the original array, allowing a range update to be applied in O(1) by only touching the two endpoints.

diff[left] += value marks 'start adding value from here.' diff[right + 1] -= value marks 'stop adding it after here.' Each range update touches only TWO positions -- O(1) -- no matter how large the range is. The final prefix-sum pass (recall Prefix Sum's exact running-total technique) reconstructs the true values in one O(n) pass.

sections.title: Tracing the reconstruction
steps: Final result: [0, 5, 8, 8, 3, 3] -- matches applying both updates directly!

sections.text: Starting from all zeros: adding 5 to [1,3] gives [0,5,5,5,0,0]. Then adding 3 to [2,5] gives [0,5,8,8,3,3]. This matches the difference array's reconstructed result exactly -- confirming the technique.

sections.title: Direct range update vs difference array, for q updates on n elements
Verifying by hand
points: This matches the difference array's reconstructed result exactly

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the diff array needs ONE EXTRA slot (size n+1) to safely mark right+1 even when right is the last valid index.
Forgetting the final reconstruction step -- the diff array alone is NOT the answer; you must run a prefix-sum pass over it to get final values.
Mixing up which index gets +value and which gets -value -- it's always diff[left] += value and diff[right + 1] -= value.
Using a difference array for a problem that also needs range-sum QUERIES between updates -- difference array only helps when you can apply ALL updates first and read the final result afterward.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What problem does a difference array solve?
questions.answer: Applying many range updates (adding a value to every element in a range) efficiently -- each update costs O(1) by only touching two positions, instead of O(range length) to update every element directly.

questions.question: How do you apply a range update [left, right] with value v using a difference array?
questions.answer: diff[left] += v and diff[right + 1] -= v -- marking where the addition starts and where it should stop, without touching anything in between.

questions.question: How do you get the final array values from a difference array?
questions.answer: Run a prefix sum pass over the difference array itself -- result[i] = result[i-1] + diff[i] -- which reconstructs the cumulative effect of every applied update.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A difference array is the mirror image of prefix sum: instead of answering range-sum queries quickly, it applies range UPDATES quickly. Adding a value v to every element in [left, right] only requires diff[left] += v and diff[right + 1] -= v -- O(1) per update, regardless of range size. After all updates are applied, a single prefix-sum pass over the difference array reconstructs the final values in O(n). This turns q range updates from O(q*n) worst case down to O(q + n) total, as long as you only need the final result after all updates, not intermediate query answers.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Difference array = mark the START (+value) and the STOP (-value) of an effect, then let a prefix-sum pass 'fill in' everything in between. Prefix Sum answers range QUERIES fast; Difference Array applies range UPDATES fast -- mirror images of each other.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What problem does a difference array solve, and how does it relate to prefix sum?
How do you apply a range update [left, right] with value v to a difference array?
How do you reconstruct final array values from a difference array?
Why does the difference array need one extra slot beyond the original array's size?
